A question I might want to ask the people in the audience there is, VIP is going anteriorly here, which is what I do, but I still see surgeons going posteriorly. and the rest. So I would say about sixty to sixty five percent Peter are doing exactly what's being done now and about thirty percent plus is the posterior approach. But can I just offer the opinion that I think you should learn both and be situationalist if the pouch of Douglas is wide open I'll take it and I must admit trainees prefer learning posterior, it's just quicker for them. But I make them learn anterior eventually. And sometimes the posterior space is terrible. And so I wouldn't stick to one all the time.

in facilitating their transition to be able to do the posterior bladder neck and the pedicles more easily. This is an XI system. I should point out that the issue with the XI is the camera and the color. And Intuitive has largely fixed that, and we're testing a new camera, which will be available, we think. The surgeon has defatted the prostate within approximately six minutes, using robotic arms similar to the SI system but with the XI camera features that allow toggling up and down for enhanced visualization.

The surgeon has completed the dissection to take the bladder down, revealing the prostate. The prostate has been defatted, and the anterior fat is being addressed. The surgeon's precise movements are visible as they prepare specimens for permanent section.

So, so far he's used a zero degree lens, and I think he's going to go for the thirty down at this point. Yeah, you can talk to them now, Peter, if you want. They have the camera on you. Yeah, so I think the important points right now is the fat in the anterior part of the prostate is very important. It allows you to identify the bladder neck. I do think sending that off is important. We think it's important because you will sometimes identify lymph nodes. On occasion, they'll be positive, but other lymph nodes will be negative. So I like the idea. I generally go thirty up, thirty down. Peter, you were with me in Brisbane recently, and the Australians were talking about the idea that they've completely abandoned doing node dissections on anybody. what are the current indications for a node dissection in someone like this? Can I just, one second. Before I start, if I can say something before I operate. This is why we're going to do it differently.

The surgeon begins by opening the endopelvic fascia minimally to perform a retrograde nerve-sparing technique. The puboprostatic ligaments are taken down, and the apex is isolated. A DVC stitch is placed due to the tumor's location, and the anterior bladder neck is dissected while preserving nerve integrity. The apex is left for later dissection to ensure full control.

The surgeon manipulates the small prostate using the third robotic arm, lifting back and up to accentuate the hump, followed by the squeeze technique. The surrounding fat has been removed, revealing the prostate's contours.

The surgeon performs lateral dissections before proceeding with the anterior dissection, using electrocautery at a low setting. The surgeon inspects the bladder interior for ureters if necessary, conducting a quick surveillance from the inside out.

The surgeon identifies the circular fibers of the bladder neck and proceeds with the dissection towards the prostate. The transition from circular to vertical longitudinal fibers indicates proximity to the prostate and seminal vesicles. Care is taken to avoid buttonholing the bladder during this step.

The surgeon identifies longitudinal fibers of the posterior bladder plate, indicating the proximity to the seminal vesicles (SVs), which are visible on the opposite side. The prostate volume is noted to be between twenty-five and thirty cubic centimeters, with a visible tumor measuring 1.7 centimeters.

I bet you never really missed a case. Yeah, but I don't think anybody can see it. I don't know. We'll show it later. So why did you change your approach or not ligating the DVC at the beginning? You think this can improve functional outcomes? What is your opinion in this technical modification? The key is the anterior approach and not ligating the apex. Yeah, I think, you know, here he's got an apical tumor. I don't think we've switched. I think I vary the operation based upon where the location of the tumor and what I need to do.

The surgeon cauterizes the detrusor muscle, causing visible tissue separation. As the dissection progresses laterally, clips are applied to maintain a dry surgical field and avoid bleeding from the plexus. The assistant skillfully identifies and utilizes a small anatomical window for further dissection, while the surgeon takes down the lateral pedicles before addressing the ampulla.

What do you mean by an extra port, Peter? You still have six or you have seven, you mean? So I have, you're holding up with your third arm. Yeah. So I use the third arm to hold up my bladder. Sure. And my assistant holds up the catheter. Got it. Okay. So six ports though, same. Got it. What do you think about the fibers back here and, you know, the vasculature? I think this is kind of an important location not to pin out the bladder or use too much cautery in some of these areas. Yeah, I think when you dissect out the ampulla, I think you can use cautery when you get it away from the base of the semivessicle. That medial vessel you have now on that, I will usually use my bipolar on that. But you're taking it down nicely. So I think that essentially there you're fine.

The surgeon identifies the tip of the left seminal vesicle and opens the space around it to loosen the prostate, enhancing mobility for dissection. Clips are applied to secure the area, ensuring the seminal vesicles are controlled before addressing the ampulla.
The surgeon identifies the seminal vesicle on the right side, avoiding cauterization in the deep lateral areas while applying clips laterally, which are crucial for the anterior approach.

So that's the main learning curve on the anterior approach is creating the space by getting some lateral bladder pedicle down. Did someone say the MRI is ready? Yeah, we can go ahead and show it, and if you need me to pause, I can. Let me know if you can do both. Let's see the, go ahead, comment. Samir? Oh, Raphael? No. Yeah, it'd be great if Samir and Phil could take a look. Go ahead. So this is a T-two weighted image. I'm not a radiologist, but I stayed at sort of a Holiday Inn Express last night. So that looks like a T-two weighted abnormality that would be high suspicion. If you had that image in isolation, you couldn't tell that that is...

So that's the problem with T-two weighted images alone. But presuming that is cancer, then you notice two things as you scroll up and down a little bit on that. You can see that there's a little bit of capsular bulge The yellow line is obscuring your vision a little bit. Scroll the other way now, and you'll see that there's a little bit of capsular irregularity underlying it. You can see there's a sharp line black line beneath the abnormality, and then you sort of lose the capsule a little bit along the neurovascular bundle on that side. So those things are both associated with extracapsular extension. Capsular irregularity, capsular bulge, and the length of the capsular contact is also associated with the likelihood of ECE. So looking at that, that's roughly five or six millimeters of capsular contact on the posterolateral.

So if you had a diffusion image, that might also give you a better sense of how much capsular contact there is. than the T two weighted image alone. So for practical decision-making purposes, does this mean you do non nerve sparing around that or you just do sort of a low release, you know, a nerve spare with a little bit of margin, if that makes sense. Yeah. My own practice is to utilize the MRI to decide where I need a wider extra fascial dissection. Uh, and I think you can pretty reliably do that. Not, not only just in terms of the lobe, But in terms of the actual location, you can have a wider extra fascial plane along the apex here and still stay relatively close along the mid gland and base, recognizing that the MRI probably under predicts the intrapastatic volume of disease by about a centimeter on average.

If I can add something, the problem is, according to the big review that has been published in European Urology, that the sensitivity is fifty-seven percent for extracapsular extension, particularly when you really need to make this question, because, of course, if you have a palpable disease, and four TPSAs is easy. But when the situation is so-and-so, it seems that if the disease seems out of the prostate on the MRI, it's fine. otherwise its borderline is so difficult to understand. I agree. And I always say that I'm not sure MRI has greatly improved as a staging modality, although its specificity is better when you fold in the diffusion and the other parameters. Yeah, and again, the implications, Samir, this is a forty-eight-year-old gentleman, and positive margins all of a sudden will be a major issue, you know, with an expected survival of forty more years, you know, it's a tough one.

The surgeon identifies Denonvilliers' fascia laterally, creating a plane without cauterizing or cutting, as an artery is present. Once released on both sides, the prostate visibly lifts up by another centimeter or more.

The surgeon performs an intrafascial dissection to release the nerve bundles from below, utilizing the XI system. The endopelvic fascia remains unopened initially, with plans to open it later. The artery serves as a landmark during the dissection.

It's trickier. One of the advantages of the VipPad is on the X side, you can get the camera in the right place and flip it. If you're coming from the bladder, you're going to slime yourself more often than not. I've done it, but it's just not as smooth as this. But sometimes with a zero lens, you can do part of this and then just flip around to the anterior lateral and finish it the other way. But XI, you can really get extreme. We did this with Vip on a cadaver lab over at Intuitive. at a master class. I must admit the cadaver doesn't quite do it justice, but at least you get the right plane. You don't get the blood vessel feedback, but the point we did in the cadaver lab that's worth pointing out is that sometimes on the monitor you're not quite oriented about where he is.

The surgeon performs a posterior release between the apex and base of the prostate, starting at the mid-gland. They leave a few fascial layers intact on one side, opting for a slightly wider dissection on the left apical area.

This is the part where the patient explanation is always tricky because patients tend to have this binary, did you spare my nerves or not, kind of question. Sometimes they write it and have the spouse ask you that when you get to the waiting room after. And so someone gave me this analogy of grapefruit analogy of, Yeah, we spared your nerves. The question is, did you peel the grapefruit inside the rind, right on the fruit, or outside the grapefruit? I use an onion, because an onion has several layers of connective tissue, so people can, or peel, so they can get that a little better. We peeled the onion really close, or we took some of the peel with us, but we still spared those nerves. Hey, what would you do now?
The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia at the base of the prostate, aiming to develop the posterior plane. The smaller size of the prostate allows for easier maneuvering compared to larger tumors.

The surgeon observes the retrograde nerves, which have been released, with the capsule remaining intact. A thin fascial layer is visible on top of the capsule. Clips are placed while leaving the lateral prostatic fascia intact.

The surgeon demonstrates a technique at the base of the pedicle, highlighting the proximity of the nerve bundle to the prostate base. The prostate is elevated to avoid accidental clipping of nerve bundles during the procedure.

Well, that's one of the comments. And I think Dr. Rivieta is in the audience. He was with me. He was a co-fellow with you. And this was the big question. It's not about whether you preserve it or not. It's how much. And he said, you know, in Latin America, it's a little bit different in that you're starting the DVC dissection very proximal to the base of the prostate, whereas I tend to still go somewhat distal under the dorsal vein. I don't like to start too proximal. Maybe if he's in the audience, he can explain the discussion we had. Which doctor? Which one did he say, Rafael? Marcelo. Oh, Marcelo. I don't think he's there. Look, there he is.
The surgeon carefully peels off tissue near the bladder neck, preserving the neurovascular bundle while releasing the dorsal venous complex (DVC) underneath without ligating it.

I think you can improve functional outcomes. We just published in European Neurology a small series, about a hundred and thirty patients that we have shown that early continence seems to be much better. We have like eighty-five percent of patients with recovery continence in the first month after surgery. So I really think that it's sparing these anterior fibers. Yeah, I go right there. I tend to go over here. I don't like to leave this anterior. I'm sure, you know, I think it makes a difference potentially in margins. So, like I said, I left the lateral prosthetic fascia. I do open the endopelvic. I just do it in a different way now. If you have the chance to see, you know, the handful of really good folks doing retzius sparing surgery, you know, they come from underneath this plane, come over it, and they leave the whole shelf of DVC intact.

The surgeon approaches the dorsal vein complex (DVC) mid-prostate, angling forward towards the urethra. The technique involves preserving the anterior notch and achieving a tight closure of the DVC. The fascia appears more pink, and the surgeon leaves additional fascia intact to maintain urethral length and preserve peripheral nerves.

I think this may be a better way to preserve some of the peripheral nerves that come in that we might be injuring if we go deep with the endopelvic dissection. Well, I think when you spare it like this, you're less likely, you know, the nerve supply that's the most important, of course, is below the endopelvic fascia. But when you do it like this, you're less likely to get into it. All right, let's put the stitch, Kevin. Go ahead. So Peter, what do you think at this stage? So this is a beautiful dissection. You know, my turn comes, I want mine to look like this. I tend to go through this sharply. I'll be doing the same. What I do is it kind of gets messy and bloody.

But I tend to go over here and leave all this. I like to have this cushion. Obviously with anterior tumors, it's a little bit different, but I like to have that cushion. I don't like to go here. I like to go over here. And so for me, it bleeds. And so this is, This is the reason I'm putting this where some of the guys probably wouldn't. So what are your thoughts on functional outcomes with this technical modification? I think early confidence is better when you were able to do this kind of dissection. We're still looking at it. I think we've done like fifty this way and it's been good. I mean the guys have been coming back and I can tell you that the fellows and the clinic staff have been impressed with how these guys have been doing and so I think Now we have a good database, and the database, we've been following them carefully. They've been doing very well.

So I will usually put this stitch in, leave it, and then go back and finish it off after I cut through this. And it's really just to keep it a little drier and cleaner. Peter, I know you've seen Vip do this several times and probably other surgeons, so I'll just ask the question I'm sure someone else in the audience is thinking is, how come it never bleeds when Vip does this? So maybe, Peter, could you address, through Vip, either way you want to do it, what's the difference between technical nuances that keep it this dry versus those that are less dry? Well, a couple of things. This is an ideal patient based on age, T-stage, prostate volume, no previous radiation. So you have great exposure here. You have large prostate, older patient, surgery, these things. But I always say that I've done a lot of cancer surgery in my lifetime, but I think prostate detecting is the most humbling because it can go beautifully for several and you can struggle.

The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field and discusses the approach to preserving the dorsal venous complex (DVC) by staying closer to the bladder neck, avoiding ligation of the DVC, and leaving the needle aside for later use.

The surgeon uses a quill suture to sew through the veins, minimizing cautery to maintain visibility. The veins are visible above, and the apex is identified, indicating where to begin the downward dissection.

The surgeon adjusts the prostate margin, leaving a couple of millimeters of tissue on the prostate while preparing the margin. The catheter is not visible, indicating a need for reinforcement during dissection.

Yeah, we still do the suspension when I do a regular case, normal case, which is a bigger tumor, larger prostate on this guy. And on those, obviously, I've taken down the puboprostatics, pubourethral's gone back. Here, I think it's still suspended, so I don't need to. it would be kind of weird to try and take this and suspend it. Yeah. It would be difficult.

Yeah, I might ask Samir, you know, there's some work on the measuring the urethral length on MR. Some reported, you can certainly do it, and some reported a, favorable relationship. We didn't see that, but Samir, you may want to comment on measuring MRI and measuring if you reach a length. Yeah, I agree with you. It can be done. The literature is somewhat mixed on that of whether it's truly predictive or not. And I think the reason for that may be technique related as well. If you have a very large gland with a fat apex, The membranous urethra in the dependent position is going to be sort of flattened out and look short. But once you release the apex and stretch the prostate, the urethra may be quite long. So I think it ends up being more what length of urethra you're preserving than what length you see preoperatively.

We've looked at when people have ECE, it tends to be one centimeter or less. In a case like this, it'd be... I think we just go a little further here. I don't think it makes any deficit if I go here versus here. No, no. Take it down just a little different. All right. Take a look at this vessel up here. Left a little bit, like I said, on this side. Yeah, very nice. This is where I went, did the full. Here, I think we left a little bit more. See right here, we left, we went, we left a little bit of the vein here on this side, just to give us a little bit of cap cushion.

Peter, if you had an MRI with the comments that were made about extraprostatic extension, how would that affect your decision-making on the lymph node dissection? I think we have a talk on lymphadenectomy today? Yes, we do, right? Okay, so maybe we can talk about that then.
The surgeon observes the separated nerve bundles within the prostatic fascia, noting that the 'train tracks' appearance has disappeared as the bundles are buried within the lateral prostatic fascia.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for bleeding around the nerve bundles and uses a suture to close veins without cautery or clips, aiming to maintain an athermal approach. The bladder neck is closed using a fish mouth technique with a quill suture.

The surgeon performs posterior reconstruction, preserving the lateral prostatic fascia. The cutting edge is used to isolate and divide the posterior part of the rhabdo sphincter, clearly revealing the anatomy and remnants to be joined.

The surgeon performs a posterior reconstruction using the Rocco stitch technique, incorporating the suture into the posterior anastomosis to join structures together for enhanced hemostasis and robust anastomosis.

The surgeon reconstructs the posterior plate by bringing together the cut edge of Denonvilliers' fascia, which was removed with the prostate. The bladder is then brought down to the urethra to complete the anastomosis, achieving hemostasis and reducing oozing.

The surgeon refines the posterior part of the anastomosis by passing stitches through the posterior urethral edge twice, joining the remnants of the bladder neck and posterior urethra. This technique aims to improve the results on posterior leakage.

I keep the catheter four to five days. We're in there. We don't do cystograms on everybody, just internationals usually since they're traveling. We leave a drain. It's important for me. We have an experienced team, so if these guys go to recovery and start putting a lot out of their drain, they'll call me to come take a look at the patient because they know it's not normal. And once a year, without fail almost, there's one guy who, the best case of the day you did and either the foot pops off or something weird happens and you can see he's just, he's oozing, he's bleeding, and you just take him back, fix it, and it's taken me a lot of headaches. So I like to leave the drain, I just take it out the next day.

Peter and John, here's a forty eight year old gentleman, would you have done a node dissection? Remind me, he was biopsy scleason six, but actually because of the MRI and the comments, I probably would have considered it. But what was his biopsy score again? Was he a six? I don't know, that's a tough one. I mean, he's probably not going to be a six if he's got an MRI with those comments. I mean... So you'd almost have to project that he's a seven. I think that this is a very nice interesting point. We might have the chance to discuss after. I will talk this, I will give a talk on this topic. I think that's the reason of complications mainly on robotic prostatectomy is lymph node dissection. So I personally like to do it only in the patients where it's very, very strictly indicated and possibly avoid it because otherwise, you know, it's time consuming and, you know.

So there may be cases with favorable intermediate to low grade where we'll skip lymph nodes and tell them there's two or three percent chance you may have to do it as a secondary procedure. But I think having salvaged enough outside cases, the worst thing you can do I think is get into the obturator fossa and pluck out two nodes and quit. Because if someone ever has to come behind you, that'll take two hours aside potentially to undo all that. So if you're going to get in the obturator fossa, finish it. Get it all. Access is easy doing salvage. The hard part is coming behind a standard. So, I mean, that's actually what European guidelines say is if you're going to do it, do it all, otherwise don't do it. Yeah. Right. Right? I mean, so I guess the standard dissection probably needs to kind of fade away. We've looked at some data recently where I've always believed that maybe we could use the PI-RADS score to guide our decisions regarding node dissection, particularly in patients with three plus three and three plus four, but it doesn't seem to pan out just yet.

We'll just have a discussion about the anatomy. Peter, what do you think of the final anatomy? Excellent discussion. Good preservation of the, I think, of the anterior urethral support. Good urethral preservation here. Tight in the asthenosis. Excellent. Your prediction on how this guy will do?
